body:
## Summary

When a long-running session with `thinking: high` (or any extended thinking mode) approaches context limits and triggers auto-compaction, the message sanitization pipeline modifies thinking block signatures on retained messages. This causes Anthropic to reject all subsequent API calls with:

```
thinking/redacted_thinking blocks cannot be modified
```

The session becomes **unrecoverable** — every subsequent turn fails with the same error until the session is manually cleared.

## Environment

- **OpenClaw version:** 2026.3.2
- **Model:** anthropic/claude-opus-4-6
- **Thinking level:** high
- **Context usage at failure:** ~81% (161k/200k tokens)

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Configure a session with `thinking: high` and a model with extended thinking (e.g., claude-opus-4-6)
2. Use the session over an extended period with many tool calls (the session will accumulate many thinking blocks with signatures)
3. Continue until context usage approaches the compaction threshold (~80%+)
4. Auto-compaction triggers → subsequent API calls fail

## Root Cause Analysis

In the Pi embedded agent message preparation pipeline, when building messages for the Anthropic API:

- `preserveSignatures` is set to `false` for direct Anthropic connections
- `dropThinkingBlocks` is correctly `false` (thinking blocks are kept)
- However, the sanitization pipeline can modify thinking block content or strip signatures when `preserveSignatures: false`

When auto-compaction runs:
1. Old messages are summarized into a compaction summary
2. Recent messages are retained
3. The retained messages go through the sanitization pipeline
4. Thinking block signatures on retained messages are modified/stripped
5. Anthropic API rejects the request because thinking blocks must be preserved exactly as returned

### Secondary Factor

This may also be triggered by mid-session OpenClaw upgrades if the thinking block serialization format changes between versions.

## Expected Behavior

- Thinking blocks (including `thinking`, `redacted_thinking`, and their `signature`/`thinkingSignature` fields) on retained messages should be preserved **exactly as returned by the API** through compaction and sanitization
- `preserveSignatures` should be `true` for Anthropic when thinking is enabled
- If signatures cannot be preserved, the affected messages should be fully dropped (included in compaction summary) rather than sent with corrupted signatures

## Workarounds

1. Periodically `/reset` long sessions before context reaches compaction threshold
2. Set thinking level to `adaptive` instead of `high` to reduce thinking block accumulation
3. Manually clear the session when the error occurs

## Impact

**High** — This is a silent data corruption bug that makes sessions unrecoverable. Users with `thinking: high` on long sessions will inevitably hit this. The only fix is manual session clearing, which loses all conversation context.
title: Bug: compaction corrupts thinking block signatures, causing unrecoverable session failure

body:
## Summary
The browser docs did not clearly describe how to connect OpenClaw to an already-running Chrome instance via `--remote-debugging-port` (without relying on extension-tab attachment).

## Why this matters
Users who want to reuse existing Chrome login sessions (or run automation without per-tab extension clicks) need an explicit, supported configuration path.

## Proposed/implemented improvement
Add a dedicated section in the browser docs (English + Chinese) covering:
- launching Chrome with `--remote-debugging-port`
- configuring a CDP profile (`cdpUrl`) in `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json`
- using `attachOnly: true`
- loopback-only security guidance for the debugging endpoint

Implemented by PR: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/36223
title: docs(browser): clarify existing Chrome remote-debugging setup

body:
## Problem

When a conversation reaches the token limit and OpenClaw switches to a fallback AI model, messages that have already been posted and displayed in the UI are being deleted or replaced mid-display. 

This means:
- A message appears in the chat
- While reading it, it disappears and is replaced by the next message
- The original content is lost and unrecoverable
- Crucial information can be missed

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Have a long conversation that approaches the context/token limit
2. OpenClaw switches to a fallback model to conserve context
3. Observe that previously posted messages are being deleted or overwritten in real time

## Expected Behaviour

Once a message has been posted and displayed in the UI, it should be immutable. Context conservation strategies (summarisation, truncation, etc.) should happen silently in the background and never alter or remove messages that have already been rendered to the user.

If messages must be removed from the active context, they should:
1. Remain visible in the UI with a visual indicator (e.g. "archived" or "summarised")
2. Never disappear silently while the user is reading them

## Impact

This is a significant issue in production use:
- Users cannot trust that what they are reading will remain on screen
- Critical information exchanged during a session can be silently lost
- There is no way to retrieve the deleted content after the fact

## Environment

- OpenClaw gateway (Docker)
- Browser-based Control UI
- Occurs when context limit is reached and fallback model is triggered
title: [Bug] Messages deleted/replaced mid-display when switching to fallback model on context limit

body:
## Problem
When spawning subagents via `sessions_spawn`, the subagent has no access to the parent session's skill list. It only receives the task prompt, so any skill-specific workflows must be manually transcribed into the spawn prompt — which leads to omissions and inconsistencies.

## Example
Parent session has `bid-review` skill with a detailed two-round image review strategy (Sonnet initial scan + Opus deep analysis). When spawning a subagent for bid review, the skill's workflow was manually summarized in the task prompt, but the two-round cost optimization strategy was omitted, resulting in 5x higher costs.

## Proposed Solution
Allow subagents to inherit the parent session's `<available_skills>` list (or a subset), so the subagent can:
1. See which skills are available
2. Read the relevant SKILL.md on its own
3. Follow the skill's workflow without manual transcription

### Options
- **Full inheritance**: subagent gets the same skill list as parent
- **Selective**: spawn option like `skills: ['bid-review', 'md-to-pdf-cjk']` to pass specific skills
- **Auto-match**: subagent gets skill descriptions injected, matches on its own

## Impact
- Reduces prompt engineering errors in spawn tasks
- Ensures subagents follow established workflows
- Prevents cost/quality issues from missed optimization steps
title: Feature: subagent skill inheritance

body:
## Problem

When the agent is busy processing a message, subsequent incoming messages are queued and delivered as `[Queued messages while agent was busy]` in the next turn. 

The `message_id` in queued messages is an internally generated placeholder (e.g. `om_x100b55ac...` with an `x` prefix) rather than the original platform message ID. This means the agent cannot use the platform API to reply directly to the queued message (e.g. Feishu's `im.message.reply` returns 400 for these IDs).

## Current Workaround

- Use `im.message.create` (send to chat) instead of `im.message.reply` for queued messages
- This loses the reply-to-specific-message context

## Proposed Solution

Preserve and pass through the original platform `message_id` in queued message metadata, so the agent can still use platform-native reply APIs.

## Environment

- OpenClaw 2026.2.24
- Feishu plugin
- Observed in group chats with high message frequency
title: Queued messages should preserve original platform message_id

body:
## Summary

Allow operators to inject a custom system prompt from an external file path specified in config, applied to all sessions, and not editable by the agent.

## Problem to solve

Operators who run openclaw-based agents need to enforce behavioral instructions (guardrails, persona, operational rules) via the system prompt. The current options each have a gap:

| Current option | Gap |
|---|---|
| **Workspace bootstrap files** (AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, etc.) | Agent can overwrite these files at runtime, so they cannot reliably enforce instructions |
| **Channel-level `systemPrompt`** in config | Only applies to group/channel sessions — does not cover DM or main sessions |
| **Hardcoding in source** (system-prompt.ts) | Requires code changes and redeployment for every prompt iteration |
| **Gateway API `extraSystemPrompt`** | Requires programmatic API calls per request — not a static config |

There is no way to say "inject this text into every session's system prompt" from config alone, in a way the agent cannot modify.

## Proposed solution

Add a new config field:

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "systemPromptFile": "/path/to/system-prompt.md"
    }
  }
}
```

**Behavior:**
- On session start, read the file at the specified path and inject its contents into the system prompt
- The file path is absolute or relative to the config file location
- The file is outside the agent's workspace, so the agent cannot edit it
- Applied to **all** session types (DM, group, main, subagent)
- Injected alongside (not replacing) existing bootstrap files and channel-level `systemPrompt`
- If the file does not exist or is unreadable, log a warning and continue without injection (fail-open, same as missing bootstrap files)

**Injection point:** The natural place is the existing `extraSystemPrompt` path — the same mechanism used by channel-level `systemPrompt` and the gateway API. This keeps the injection consistent and minimizes code changes.

## Alternatives considered

1. **`systemPrompt` as inline string in config** — Works for short prompts but impractical for longer instructions. JSON escaping of multiline Markdown is painful. Operators who version-control and build their prompts need file-based input.
2. **Protecting workspace files from agent writes** (e.g., per-file read-only) — Would solve the "agent can overwrite" problem, but openclaw currently only supports workspace-wide `workspaceAccess: "ro"`, which blocks all agent writes. Per-file protection would require significant plumbing (mount-level or fs-bridge changes). The file-based approach sidesteps this entirely.
3. **`bootstrap-extra-files` hook** — Can inject additional workspace files, but they remain in the workspace and are editable by the agent (unless `workspaceAccess: "ro"`, which is too broad).
4. **Custom `bootstrapFiles` config** (#16971, #17965, PR #15568, PR #31901) — These proposals add custom files to the bootstrap set, but bootstrap files live in the workspace and are agent-editable. They solve "what files to inject" but not "prevent agent from modifying them."

## Impact

- **Affected:** Operators running always-on agents (Slack, Discord, Telegram bots) who need to enforce behavioral rules
- **Severity:** Medium — workarounds exist (hardcoding in source, or accepting that agents may overwrite instructions) but they are fragile or high-maintenance
- **Frequency:** Every prompt iteration cycle — operators update behavioral instructions regularly as they observe agent misbehavior
- **Consequence:** Without this, operators must either accept that agents can silently modify their own instructions, or maintain a fork with hardcoded prompt changes

## Evidence/examples

- Channel-level `systemPrompt` already exists in config for Slack (`channels.slack.channels.<id>.systemPrompt`), Telegram, Discord, Google Chat, and IRC — proving the pattern works. This proposal extends the same concept to all sessions via `agents.defaults`.
- Related feature requests for custom bootstrap files: #16971, #17965
- Related PRs implementing custom bootstrap files (workspace-based): #15568, #31901
- The gateway API already accepts `extraSystemPrompt` as a parameter, showing the injection path exists in the codebase.

## Additional information

- This is backward-compatible: omitting `systemPromptFile` preserves current behavior
- The file is read at session start, so changes to the file take effect on the next session without redeployment
- Operators can build prompt management pipelines (version control, templating, CI/CD) that output to this file path — openclaw just needs to read it

✍️ Author: Claude Code with @carrotRakko (AI-written, human-approved)
title: [Feature]: agents.defaults.systemPromptFile — file-based system prompt injection from config

body:
## Bug

The `message` tool's media path validation uses `getDefaultMediaLocalRoots()` instead of `getAgentScopedMediaLocalRoots()`, so files under the agent's configured workspace directory are rejected with `LocalMediaAccessError: path-not-allowed`.

## Reproduction

- Agent workspace: `/home/rav/clawd` (configured in `agents.defaults.workspace`)
- File to send: `/home/rav/clawd/daycare-enrolment-form.html`
- Channel: WhatsApp
- Result: `Local media path is not under an allowed directory`

## Expected

`getAgentScopedMediaLocalRoots(cfg, agentId)` in `src/media/local-roots.ts` already appends the agent workspace to the allowed roots. But the message/send tool path doesn't call it — it uses `getDefaultMediaLocalRoots()` which only includes `~/.openclaw/media`, `~/.openclaw/agents`, `~/.openclaw/workspace`, `~/.openclaw/sandboxes`, and the preferred tmp dir.

## Workaround

Copy files to `~/.openclaw/media/outbound/` before sending.

## Version

OpenClaw 2026.3.2 (85377a2)
title: message tool uses default media roots instead of agent-scoped roots

body:
### Bug type

Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

### Summary

`chat.inject` returns:                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                                                                                                                 
   `failed to write transcript: transcript file not found`                                                                                                                       
                                                                                                                                                                                 
   when the resolved `transcriptPath` exists in session metadata but the transcript file itself does **not** exist on disk (ENOENT). Manually creating (touching) the transcript 
 file makes the error go away immediately.                                                                                                                                       
                                                                                                                                                                                 
   This breaks transcript persistence and makes `chat.history` / `sessions_history` return empty. 

### Steps to reproduce

1. Configure OpenClaw with ACP backend `acpx` and run an ACP oneshot/run session (nonInteractive).                                                                            
   2. Observe the session entry has a `transcriptPath` like:                                                                                                                     
      `~/.openclaw/agents/<agentId>/sessions/<sessionId>.jsonl`                                                                                                                  
      but the file does **not** exist (ENOENT).                                                                                                                                  
   3. Call gateway RPC `chat.inject` with params:                                                                                                                                
      `{ "sessionKey": "<sessionKey>", "message": "hello" }`                                                                                                                     
   4. Observe error:                                                                                                                                                             
      `failed to write transcript: transcript file not found`                                                                                                                    
   5. Workaround: `touch` the transcript file at `transcriptPath`, then retry `chat.inject`; it succeeds and messages are persisted.

### Expected behavior

If `transcriptPath` is resolved but the file is missing, `chat.inject` should create the transcript file (mkdirp + create/touch) and then append the injected message, instead of failing. 

### Actual behavior

Source inspection (OpenClaw 2026.3.2) shows `chat.inject` calls the transcript append helper with `createIfMissing: false`, so a missing transcript file becomes a hard error. 

### OpenClaw version

2026.3.2

### Operating system

macOS arm64

### Install method

brew (openclaw-cli) + LaunchAgent gateway  

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```shell
File: `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`                                                                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                                 
   - `appendAssistantTranscriptMessag e(...)` returns `"transcript file not found"` if `createIfMissing` is false and the transcript file does not exist.                        
   - RPC method `"chat.inject"` calls `appendAssistantTranscriptMessag e` with:                                                                                                  
     `createIfMissing: false`  
 ### Proposed fix                                                                                                                                                              
                                                                                                                                                                                 
   In `chat.inject`, pass `createIfMissing: true` (or ensure transcript file is created at session creation / first write). Missing transcript file should not hard-fail         
 injection.
```

### Impact and severity

 - Affected: ACP oneshot/run sessions where transcript is not pre-created                                                                                                      
   - Severity: High (breaks persistence/observability; history APIs unusable)                                                                                                    
   - Frequency: 100% when transcript file is missing                                                                                                                             
   - Consequence: chat history remains empty; tooling depending on transcript breaks 

### Additional information

_No response_
title: [Bug]:  chat.inject fails when transcript file is missing (createIfMissing=false)

body:
Hey OpenClaw folks! 👋

We've been building something cool on top of OpenClaw and wanted to share it with the community — plus some Matrix plugin improvements we've made along the way that we'd love to contribute back.

## What We Built: HiClaw

[HiClaw](https://github.com/higress-group/HiClaw) is an open-source **Agent Teams** system with Matrix-based human-in-the-loop oversight:

```
You → Manager Agent → Worker Alice (frontend)
                   → Worker Bob (backend)
                   → Worker ...
```

**Key highlights:**
- **Manager as your AI chief of staff** — creates Workers, assigns tasks, monitors progress, reports back
- **Everything in Matrix Rooms** — you see all agent-to-agent communication, can intervene anytime
- **Security by design** — Workers only hold consumer tokens, real API keys stay in Higress AI Gateway
- **Truly open IM** — self-hosted Matrix server, any client (Element, FluffyChat), mobile included
- **One-command deploy** — `curl | bash` sets up everything

We chose Matrix because it gives us **full visibility and control** over multi-agent collaboration — no black-box agent-to-agent calls, everything visible in group chat rooms where humans can jump in.

## Matrix Plugin Gaps We Hit

While building HiClaw, we ran into several issues with OpenClaw's Matrix plugin that needed fixes:

| Issue | Impact |
|-------|--------|
| [#30645](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/30645) | 2-person rooms incorrectly classified as DMs, causing duplicate messages |
| [#30650](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/30650) | MSC3916 authenticated media not supported, images/files fail on Tuwunel |
| [#30656](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/30656) | No `m.mentions` field, outbound @mentions don't trigger notifications |
| [#30662](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/30662) | No message buffering, bot has no context when @mentioned in busy rooms |
| [#30670](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/30670) | Messages during gateway restart silently dropped |

These are pretty critical for any serious Matrix-based agent system — especially the DM detection, mentions, and message buffering issues.

## Our Fix

We've implemented fixes for all of these in our fork and submitted as a consolidated PR:

👉 **[#30679](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/30679)**

The PR currently has merge conflicts because some issues got picked up by other contributors in parallel. We're happy to rebase and resolve conflicts if there's interest in merging.

## Ask

We'd love to **upstream these fixes** so we don't have to maintain a fork long-term. If any maintainers could take a look at the issues and PR, we'd appreciate it! Happy to split the PR into smaller chunks if that helps review.

Thanks for building OpenClaw — it's been a solid foundation for what we're building! 🙌

---

**Links:**
- HiClaw repo: https://github.com/higress-group/HiClaw
- Demo video (in README): Manager creating Workers, assigning tasks
- Discord: https://discord.gg/n6mV8xEYUF
title: [Showcase & Discussion] HiClaw: Multi-Agent Teams with Matrix-based Human-in-the-Loop — and Some Matrix Plugin Improvements We'd Like to Contribute

body:
### Summary

Add official DingTalk (钉钉) channel support for Chinese users

### Problem to solve

Currently, OpenClaw supports many messaging platforms like WhatsApp, Telegram, Discord, Slack, etc., but does not support DingTalk (钉钉), which is one of the most popular business communication platforms in China with over 600 million users.

Chinese users who prefer DingTalk for their daily communication and business workflows cannot use OpenClaw, limiting the platform's reach in the Chinese market.

Many businesses in China use DingTalk for team collaboration and would benefit from integrating AI assistants through this platform.

### Proposed solution

Add a new DingTalk channel provider to OpenClaw with the following features:

1. **DingTalk Bot Integration**
   - Use DingTalk Robot Webhook API for sending messages
   - Support receiving messages via callback/webhook
   - Implement OAuth 2.0 for user authentication (optional)

2. **Message Types**
   - Support text messages
   - Support rich text (markdown)
   - Support images and files
   - Support interactive cards (action cards)

3. **Chat Support**
   - Direct messages (1-on-1)
   - Group chats
   - @mentions in groups

4. **Configuration**
   - Add DingTalk credentials to agent config:
     - App Key (AppKey)
     - App Secret (AppSecret)
     - Agent ID
   - Support webhook URL configuration

5. **Implementation Location**
   - New provider in `src/providers/dingtalk/`
   - Similar structure to existing providers (telegram, discord)

### Alternatives considered

1. **Custom Webhook Integration**
   - Users can create their own DingTalk bot and use DingTalk's outgoing webhook feature
   - Limitation: Requires manual setup, no official support, may break with API changes
   - Not user-friendly for non-technical users

2. **Third-Party Bridge (e.g., ChatGPT-Next-Web)**
   - Use third-party tools that bridge DingTalk to OpenAI-compatible APIs
   - Limitation: Adds complexity, security concerns, not integrated with OpenClaw natively
   - Loses OpenClaw's unique features (multi-channel, automation, etc.)

3. **Only Support via Telegram/WhatsApp Proxy**
   - Ask Chinese users to use Telegram/WhatsApp instead
   - Limitation: Not practical, most Chinese users don't have these apps
   - Poor user experience for the target audience

**Why DingTalk native support is better:**
- Native integration provides better reliability and security
- Direct support means faster bug fixes and feature updates
- Better user experience with platform-specific features (e.g., DingTalk cards, mini-apps)
- Opens up the large Chinese market for OpenClaw

### Impact

Affected users/systems/channels:

Chinese users who want to use OpenClaw with DingTalk
Businesses in China using DingTalk for team collaboration
OpenClaw's potential user base in the Chinese market
Severity:

Annoying / Limiting
Currently blocks workflow for Chinese users who prefer DingTalk
Frequency:

Always - Any Chinese user trying to use DingTalk with OpenClaw
Consequence:

Users cannot interact with OpenClaw via their preferred communication platform
Limited adoption in China, one of the largest markets
Users must resort to workarounds or alternative solutions
Poor first impression for Chinese users discovering OpenClaw

### Evidence/examples

_No response_

### Additional information

body:
## Feature Request / Doc Gap

There is no documentation explaining how to connect openclaw's browser tools to an existing Chrome instance (with login sessions intact) instead of relying on the Chrome extension relay.

## Use Case

Users in environments where manually attaching tabs via the extension is impractical (e.g. running openclaw as a background service, or wanting browser automation without per-tab interaction) need a way to use Playwright-backed browser control against their real Chrome profile.

## How It Currently Works (undocumented)

1. Launch Chrome with remote debugging enabled:
   ```
   /Applications/Google\ Chrome.app/Contents/MacOS/Google\ Chrome \
     --remote-debugging-port=9222 \
     --user-data-dir=/path/to/profile
   ```

2. Add to `openclaw.json`:
   ```json
   "browser": {
     "cdpUrl": "http://127.0.0.1:9222",
     "profiles": {
       "chrome": {
         "cdpUrl": "http://127.0.0.1:9222",
         "color": "#4285F4"
       }
     }
   }
   ```

   The `profiles.chrome` override is necessary because openclaw auto-creates a built-in `chrome` profile pointing at the extension relay — manually defining it takes precedence.

3. Restart the gateway. openclaw will now connect directly via CDP without needing any tab attached via the extension.

## What Would Help

- A docs section under the browser configuration page explaining this pattern
- Ideally, a top-level `browser.attachOnly: true` mode that makes openclaw skip launching its own Chrome and connect to whatever is already listening on the configured CDP URL

Related: PR #36147 makes `color` optional so users don't hit a cryptic validation error when setting up this config.
title: docs: how to connect openclaw to an existing Chrome instance via --remote-debugging-port

body:
## Bug Description

When the LLM API returns a rate limit (429) or overload error **after tool calls have already completed** in a turn, the assistant response is completely empty/silent. No error message is shown to the user, no indication of what went wrong, and the conversation appears stuck.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Run multiple concurrent sub-agents (e.g., 5+ opus sessions) that consume API rate limit quota
2. In the main session, trigger a turn that involves tool calls (exec, read, etc.)
3. Tool calls complete successfully
4. The next LLM call (to generate text response from tool results) hits API rate limit
5. **Result**: Empty assistant response — no text, no error, conversation appears dead

## Expected Behavior

A user-facing error message like:
> ⚠️ API rate limit reached mid-turn — the model couldn't generate a response after tool calls completed. Please try again in a moment.

## Actual Behavior

Completely silent/empty assistant turn. The user sees tool call results in the UI but no assistant text. No error is logged to the chat.

## Root Cause

In `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts`, the post-run error handling only checks for context overflow errors (#26905 fix). Rate limit and overload errors that occur mid-turn (after tool calls) are not caught — the run "completes" with an embedded error and empty payloads, and the code returns `kind: "success"` with no text.

The pre-tool-call error path (`"Embedded agent failed before reply"`) handles rate limits correctly, but the mid-turn path does not.

## Environment

- OpenClaw 2026.3.3
- Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4-6
- Trigger: 5+ concurrent sub-agent sessions (opus) + main session tool calls
- Channel: Discord

## Gateway Error Log Evidence

```
[agent/embedded] embedded run agent end: isError=true error=⚠️ API rate limit reached.
[diagnostic] lane task error: lane=main error="FailoverError: ⚠️ API rate limit reached."
```

No corresponding user-facing message was delivered.

## Proposed Fix

Extend the existing #26905 context-overflow guard to also catch rate limit, overload, and generic embedded errors when no payload text was generated. PR incoming.
title: Silent empty response when LLM rate limit hits mid-turn (after tool calls)

body:
## Description

`openclaw gateway restart` and `openclaw gateway stop` fail in container environments (Docker, Podman, LXC, etc.) that lack systemd. Both commands immediately exit with:

```
Gateway service disabled.
Start with: openclaw gateway install
...
If you're in a container, run the gateway in the foreground instead of `openclaw gateway`.
```

The gateway process **is** running and listening on its configured port, but `runServiceRestart`/`runServiceStop` check `service.isLoaded()` first — which returns `false` when no systemd/launchd unit exists — and bail before attempting any action.

## Root Cause

`lifecycle-core.ts` requires the service to be "loaded" (systemd enabled / launchd registered) before proceeding with restart or stop. In containers without a service manager, `isLoaded()` always returns `false`, so both paths are blocked.

However, the gateway process already handles SIGUSR1 natively for graceful in-process restarts (via the run-loop's `onSigusr1` handler), and SIGTERM for graceful shutdown. The signal-based mechanisms work regardless of the service manager — the CLI just can't reach them.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Run OpenClaw in a Docker container (or any environment without systemd)
2. Start gateway: `openclaw gateway --port 18789`
3. In another shell: `openclaw gateway restart` → fails with "Gateway service disabled"
4. `openclaw gateway stop` → same failure, process still running

## Expected Behavior

- `openclaw gateway restart` should discover the running gateway process (via port-based PID discovery) and send SIGUSR1 for graceful restart
- `openclaw gateway stop` should discover the process and send SIGTERM for graceful shutdown
- Both should work transparently whether a service manager is present or not

## Environment

- **Container:** Docker / Podman / LXC (any environment without systemd)
- **OS inside container:** Debian, Ubuntu, Alpine, etc.
- **Gateway started via:** `openclaw gateway --port 18789` (foreground)
title: [Bug]: `openclaw gateway restart/stop` fail in containers without systemd

body:
### Bug type

Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

### Summary

When sessions_spawn targets a Google/Gemini model via either alias ("gemini") or full provider ID ("google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview"), the session returns modelApplied: true but the sub-agent actually runs on the configured fallback provider (Anthropic/Sonnet). The model override is silently ignored with zero error or warning.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Configure OpenClaw with both anthropic and google authentication profiles
2. Spawn a sub-agent: sessions_spawn({ task: "Identify yourself", model: "google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview", mode: "run" })
3. Observe: modelApplied: true is returned in the response
4. Check actual output: sub-agent identifies itself as Anthropic/Claude (e.g., "I'm Claude...")
5. Repeat with alias: sessions_spawn({ ..., model: "gemini", ... })
6. Result: Same silent fallback to Anthropic

### Expected behavior

Sub-agent is created and runs on the Google Gemini model specified. If the Google provider is unavailable or unconfigured, return an error with modelApplied: false and a clear message explaining why the override failed.

### Actual behavior

Sub-agent is created with modelApplied: true in the response, but the sub-agent actually runs on Anthropic/Claude (Sonnet or configured default).

The model override is silently ignored — no error, no warning, no log at any level. modelApplied: true is actively misleading.

### OpenClaw version

2026.3.3, commit 809f951

### Operating system

macOS 12.7.4 / Darwin 21.6.0 (x64)

### Install method

npm / development

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```shell
**Test Matrix: Model Parameter vs Actual Provider**

| Model Parameter | Intended Provider | Actual Provider | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `gemini` (alias) | Google | Anthropic | ❌ Silent fallback to Sonnet |
| `google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview` (full ID) | Google | Anthropic | ❌ Silent fallback to Sonnet |
| `grok` (alias) | xAI | xAI | ✅ Correct provider |
| `xai/grok-4-1-fast` (full ID) | xAI | xAI | ✅ Correct provider |

**Evidence:** Provider switching works correctly for xAI (both alias and full ID). Same code path, same auth profiles configured, but Google routes to fallback silently while xAI works as expected.

**Cost impact:** Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite = $1.50/MTok output. Sonnet 4.6 = $15/MTok output. Silent fallback = 10x cost increase with no visibility.

**Metadata misleading:** Session response includes `modelApplied: true` even though the actual model is different.
```

### Impact and severity

Affected: Multi-model pipelines attempting to use Google Gemini models (including Guardian screening workflows, cost-optimised deployments, and model diversity experiments).

Severity: HIGH (breaks model selection and cost control).

Frequency: 100% reproducible — occurs every time sessions_spawn targets a Google model.

Consequence: 
- Silent 10x cost increase (Gemini $1.50/MTok → Sonnet $15/MTok)
- modelApplied metadata misleading — no visibility into actual routing failure
- Model diversity experiments broken — user expects Gemini, gets Sonnet
- Guardian pipelines lose provider flexibility

### Additional information

Workaround: Explicitly using Anthropic models works correctly. Users can work around this by avoiding Google models for now.

Related: This routing bug has a significant cost impact on Guardian workflows — our testing showed we've been accidentally running on Sonnet (10x cost) while believing we were using Gemini. The fix/subagent-announce-delivery branch is already being worked on; this may be related to provider routing changes in that PR.

Tested with: anthropic:default and google:default auth profiles both configured and live.
title: [Bug]: sessions_spawn silently falls back to Anthropic (set fallback model) when targeting Google Gemini models on spawned subagent tasks.

body:
## Feature Request: Improve 429 rate limit handling with multi-profile auth rotation

### Problem

When running multiple agents (5 agents + 17 cron jobs) with multiple Anthropic auth profiles (`auth.order`), 429 rate limit errors don't trigger profile rotation **within the same run's retry loop**. The current behavior:

1. Run starts → picks profile A
2. Profile A returns 429 → retries on **same profile A** (exponential backoff: 7s, 14s...)
3. Run eventually fails after multiple retries on the same exhausted profile
4. Next new run picks profile B (if A is now in cooldown)

This means a single run can waste 1-2 minutes retrying a rate-limited profile instead of immediately switching to an available one.

**Evidence from logs** (5 agents, 3 Anthropic profiles configured):
```
11:56:47 run add53e8b → rate limit ❌ (profile A)
11:56:51 run add53e8b → rate limit ❌ (same profile, 4s later)
11:56:58 run add53e8b → rate limit ❌ (same profile, 7s later)
11:57:12 run add53e8b → rate limit ❌ (same profile, 14s later)
11:57:26 run add53e8b → rate limit ❌ (same profile, 14s later)
... continues across 3 different runs for ~2 minutes
```

### Current rate_limit cooldown behavior (from source)

The cooldown for non-billing errors is hardcoded in `calculateAuthProfileCooldownMs`:
```js
// Math.min(3600 * 1000, 60 * 1000 * 5^Math.min(errorCount-1, 3))
// 1st error: 60s, 2nd: 5min, 3rd: 25min, 4th+: 60min (capped)
```

`billingBackoffHours` config only applies to billing (402) and `auth_permanent` errors. Rate limit cooldown timing is not configurable.

### Proposed improvements

**1. Mid-run profile rotation on 429 (high value)**

When a 429 is received during a run's retry loop, check if another profile is available and switch to it before retrying on the same exhausted profile. This would eliminate the wasted retry time shown in the logs above.

**2. Configurable rate limit cooldown (nice to have)**

Expose rate limit cooldown parameters in `auth.cooldowns`, similar to how `billingBackoffHours` works for billing errors. Something like:
```json5
{
  auth: {
    cooldowns: {
      rateLimitBackoffMs: [60000, 300000, 1500000, 3600000], // per error count
      // or
      rateLimitBaseMs: 60000,
      rateLimitMultiplier: 5,
      rateLimitMaxMs: 3600000
    }
  }
}
```

Anthropic uses token bucket rate limiting (continuous per-minute refill), so the optimal cooldown depends on usage patterns. A 60s first cooldown works well for light burst scenarios, but heavy multi-agent setups may benefit from shorter initial cooldowns since the bucket starts refilling immediately.

### Environment

- OpenClaw 2026.3.2
- 5 agents, 3 Anthropic auth profiles in `auth.order`
- 17 active cron jobs
- Anthropic Claude Opus 4.6, multiple $200/month accounts
title: Feature: Mid-run profile rotation on 429 + configurable rate limit cooldown

body:
### Summary

Support configuring different AI models based on chat type (direct message vs group chat) within the same agent/channel.

### Problem to solve

Currently, the `model` field in agent configuration applies globally with no way to distinguish between DM and group chat sessions. Users who want to use a powerful model (e.g., Claude) for 1-on-1 conversations and a faster/cheaper model (e.g., MiniMax) for group chats have no native way to do this via config. The only workaround is manually issuing `/model` commands per session.

### Proposed solution

Add a `byChatType` sub-field under the model config to allow per-chat-type model overrides. Example:
"model": {
  "primary": "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6",
  "byChatType": {
    "direct": "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6",
    "group": "minimax/MiniMax-M2.1"
  }
}
This should apply at both `agents.defaults.model` and per-agent `model` levels.

### Alternatives considered

_No response_

### Impact

Affected: Users with multi-channel setups (e.g., Feishu) using both DM and group chats
Severity: Medium (blocks cost optimization workflow)
Frequency: Persistent — affects every session start
Consequence: Users either overspend on group chats or manually switch models every time

### Evidence/examples

_No response_

### Additional information

_No response_
title: [Feature]: Per-chat-type model routing (DM vs Group Chat)

body:
## Summary

When an agent uses the `message` tool with `action=react` on a WhatsApp **group** message, the reaction silently fails if the `participant` field is omitted. The tool returns `{ ok: true }` but the reaction never appears. No error in logs or tool output. DM reactions work fine.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Configure a WhatsApp channel with a group chat
2. Receive a message from a group participant
3. Agent reacts via the message tool:
   ```json
   { "action": "react", "channel": "whatsapp", "target": "<group-jid>@g.us", "message_id": "<msg-id>", "emoji": "thumbs up" }
   ```
4. `participant` is not provided (the agent has no natural way to know the sender's JID)

**Expected:** Reaction appears, or tool returns an error indicating `participant` is required for groups.

**Actual:** Tool returns `{ ok: true, added: "thumbs up" }` but reaction never appears on the message.

## Root Cause

WhatsApp group messages require a composite key `{remoteJid, id, fromMe, participant}`. Without `participant`, Baileys sends an incomplete key and WhatsApp silently ignores the reaction.

The code path:
1. `src/agents/tools/whatsapp-actions.ts` — reads `participant` as optional
2. `src/web/outbound.ts` — passes it through as-is
3. `src/web/inbound/send-api.ts` — builds Baileys message key; when `participant` is undefined for a group, the key is incomplete

**The gateway already has the data.** The ack-reaction code path (`src/web/auto-reply/monitor/ack-reaction.ts`) correctly passes `participant: params.msg.senderJid` because it has direct access to `WebInboundMessage`. The agent tool path does not.

## Why Agents Can't Work Around This

1. `participant` is in the generic `buildChannelTargetSchema()`, not in the reaction-specific schema — LLMs don't see it as relevant to reactions
2. Even when agents know about it, the sender's JID isn't reliably available in conversation context
3. Other channels handle analogous auto-population (Telegram auto-populates `messageId` from `currentMessageId` in toolContext)

## Proposed Fix

**Auto-populate `participant` from inbound context** when `action=react` targets a group JID and `participant` is omitted:

1. Thread `senderJid` (already in `WebInboundMessage`) through `MessageToolOptions` / `toolContext`
2. In `handleWhatsAppAction`, when reacting to a group (`@g.us`) with no `participant`, fall back to the inbound sender JID
3. This mirrors how `ack-reaction.ts` already correctly uses `params.msg.senderJid`

**Secondary improvements:**
- Add a description to `participant` in the schema indicating it's required for WhatsApp group reactions
- Log a warning or return a soft error when reacting to a group without `participant` resolved

## Prior Art

Previously reported as #1529 — closed due to inactivity without a fix. The root cause analysis there is identical.
title: [Bug]: WhatsApp group reactions silently fail when agent omits participant field

body:
## Objective
Execute a concrete plan to maximize autonomous/proactive reliability of main-agent orchestration while keeping security-first constraints.

## Scope
- Never-silent completion guarantees
- Spawn verification hardening completion
- Operational event-class routing policy
- Session hygiene defaults

## Workstream checklist
- [ ] **P0** Fix orphaned parent callback gap (#35791)
  - [ ] Parent receives deterministic failure/completion when child session is cleaned before runTimeout
  - [ ] Unit tests for cleanup-before-timeout and missing-run-entry path
  - [ ] Explicit observability on this path
- [ ] **P0** Land and verify fail-open hardening (#35426)
  - [ ] Merge/review complete
  - [ ] 24h soak with log reason tags tracked
- [ ] **P1** Adopt proactive event classes
  - [ ] CRITICAL => immediate push
  - [ ] IMPORTANT => immediate interim update
  - [ ] ROUTINE => hourly digest
- [ ] **P1** Session hygiene baseline
  - [ ] run-mode cleanup default delete (#35730)
  - [ ] explicit keep policy only when justified
- [ ] **P2** Deterministic continuity mode
  - [ ] session reuse only after security+test quality gate (see #20072 caveats)

## Acceptance criteria
- No silent parent-orphan after child cleanup/timeout edge cases
- STARTED state only with verifiable spawn evidence (or explicit fail-open observable path)
- User-visible update discipline aligned to event classes
- Security-first: no policy bypass, no unsafe tool escalation

## Tracking
Related: #35791 #35404 #35248
Related PRs: #35426 #35273 #35730 #29468 #20072
title: Execution Plan: main-agent autonomy/proactivity hardening (silent-stall zero-tolerance)

body:
### Bug type

Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

### Summary

When a parent session spawns a mode="run" sub-agent, completion events intermittently fail to wake the parent for a new turn, causing the pendulum to stall.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Spawn a sub-agent from a WhatsApp group session with sessions_spawn({ task: "...", mode: "run" })
2. Wait for sub-agent to complete
3. Observe parent session for auto-announce turn

### Expected behavior

Parent session automatically receives a new turn with the sub-agent's output.

### Actual behavior

Parent session stalls. Sub-agent completes successfully (visible via sessions_history) but auto-announce event is lost.

### OpenClaw version

2026.3.3, commit 809f951

### Operating system

macOS 12.7.4 / Darwin 21.6.0 (x64) [on both tested Machines, 1 and 2]

### Install method

npm / development

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```shell
**Test Results: 4 rounds across 2 independent machines**

Round 1 (orchestrator → web scout (Grok 4.1 Fast) → guardian (Gemini 3 Flash) → orchestrator):
• Machine 1 (macOS, Opus 4.6 orchestrator): ✅ auto-announce worked
• Machine 2 (macOS, Opus 4.6 orchestrator): ❌ stalled — no auto-announce

Round 2 (orchestrator → web scout (Haiku 4.5) → guardian (Gemini 3 Flash) → orchestrator):
• Machine 1 (macOS, Opus 4.6 orchestrator): ❌ stalled — no auto-announce
• Machine 2 (macOS, Opus 4.6 orchestrator): ❌ stalled — no auto-announce

Round 3 (New Guardian pipeline: orchestrator → web scout (Haiku 4.5) → guardian (Grok 4.1 Fast) → orchestrator):
• Machine 1 (macOS, Opus 4.6 orchestrator): ❌ stalled at initial spawn
• Machine 2 (macOS, Opus 4.6 orchestrator): ✅ clean run

Round 4 (Machine 2 solo spawn, Machine 1 inactive):
• Machine 1: — (not participating)
• Machine 2 (macOS, Opus 4.6 orchestrator): ❌ stalled on both steps
• Critical finding: Confirms NOT caused by multi-machine/multi-agent message interference. Solo spawn on Machine 2 still stalled despite Machine 1 being completely inactive.

**Key Findings:**
- Intermittent race condition — same code path, same config, different outcomes each run
- Occurs across different models (Grok 4.1 Fast, Haiku 4.5, Gemini 3 Flash)
- Occurs across independent machines (both affected)
- Occurs even in solo runs (Machine 1 completely silent in Round 4)
```

### Impact and severity

Affected: Multi-agent Guardian pipelines (orchestrator  → web scout  → guardian → orchestrator ). Severity: High (breaks async workflows). Frequency: Intermittent but reproducible. Consequence: Requires manual sessions_history calls to retrieve results.

### Additional information

Note: Team appears to be working on this already (fix/subagent-announce-delivery branch spotted in git fetch). Filing to confirm real-world reproduction from Guardian pipeline use case.
title: [Bug]: mode="run" sub-agent completions fail to trigger parent session turn

body:
Currently, /reasoning and other direct commands (e.g., /status, /tools health) only seem to affect the Main Session (Web Console) and do not persist or activate within Channel Sessions (like LINE OA).

This leads to user confusion and a degraded experience, as users expect commands to work where they are typing them.

Proposed Solution: Implement logic to allow channel-specific commands (/<command>) to directly toggle agent states (e.g., thinking: on/off) or execute relevant actions within the specific channel session.

Benefit: Improve user experience and consistency across different chat channels.
title: Feature Request: Enable /reasoning and other commands via LINE OA Channel

body:
## Bug type
Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

## Summary
For `api: openai-responses` on non-`openai` providers (e.g. custom `sub2api-gpt`), OpenClaw can send requests with `tools` present but `tool_choice` missing/null on the streamSimple HTTP path, leading to text-only completions and no tool call execution.

## Steps to reproduce
1. Configure a custom provider using OpenAI Responses API (example: `sub2api-gpt`, model `gpt-5.3-codex`).
2. Use OpenClaw agent path (chat/session) with built-in tools available (`exec`, `browser`, etc.).
3. Ask the agent to run a tool-required task (e.g. `use exec to run pwd`).
4. Capture upstream request payload to `/v1/responses` (mitmproxy or equivalent).
5. Observe payload and SSE response.

## Expected behavior
- Requests with non-empty `tools` should include effective `tool_choice` (at least `"auto"` when unset).
- Model should emit tool call items (`function_call` / mapped toolCall) when tool execution is needed.

## Actual behavior
- Captured request had `tools` present but `tool_choice: null`/missing on streamSimple path.
- SSE response was text-only (`response.output_text.*`) with no `function_call` items.
- Session ended with `stopReason="stop"` and no `toolUse`/`toolCall`.

## OpenClaw version
2026.3.2

## Operating system
macOS 25.3.0 (arm64)

## Install method
npm global + gateway daemon

## Logs, screenshots, and evidence
Redacted evidence from live capture:

- Outbound payload to provider `/responses`:
  - `model: gpt-5.3-codex`
  - `tools: [ ... ]` (non-empty)
  - `tool_choice: null` (missing effective value)
- SSE events only contained:
  - `response.output_text.delta`
  - no `response.output_item.*` for `function_call`

Behavior is consistent with model choosing text-only output when no effective tool choice is provided.

## Impact and severity
- Affected: users on custom OpenAI-compatible Responses providers (non-`openai` provider key).
- Severity: High (tool workflows silently fail while assistant claims it will perform tool actions).
- Frequency: Intermittent/high depending on prompt/context; reproducible in production setup.
- Consequence: missed automations, false-success UX, manual retries, increased cost/time.

## Additional information
### Confirmed root cause candidate
From code-path analysis:
- `openai-responses` + provider `openai` uses dedicated websocket stream path.
- custom providers with `api: openai-responses` often route through `streamSimple` HTTP path.
- on this path, `tool_choice` is not reliably injected/forwarded.

### Minimal safe fix proposal
In `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/extra-params.ts` (`createOpenAIResponsesContextManagementWrapper` payload interceptor), inject default tool choice only when safe:

- condition: `Array.isArray(payload.tools) && payload.tools.length > 0 && payload.tool_choice == null`
- action: `payload.tool_choice = "auto"`
- do not override when caller explicitly sets `tool_choice`

### Regression tests suggested
1. `openai-responses` + tools + unset tool_choice => payload includes `tool_choice: "auto"`
2. tools empty => no forced tool_choice
3. explicit `tool_choice` (`required`/object) => preserved

### Related context
- Closely related to #34241 (this report adds packet-level evidence + concrete patch direction).

Happy to share sanitized full request/response traces if maintainers want exact payload samples.
title: Bug: openai-responses custom providers can drop effective tool_choice on streamSimple path (no tool calls)

body:
## Problem

The `skill-creator` built-in skill has a description that reliably fails to trigger when an agent is **editing or refactoring** existing skills, rather than creating new ones from scratch.

**Current description:**
> Create or update AgentSkills. Use when designing, structuring, or packaging skills with scripts, references, and assets.

The words "packaging" and "designing" read as end-of-process / blank-slate creation activities. When an agent is mid-task refactoring an existing skill (e.g. splitting a large `SKILL.md` into `SKILL.md` + `references/REFERENCE.md`), it does not match this description against its active frame of "I am fixing [domain X] behavior" and skips the skill entirely.

## Observed consequence

In a recent session, an agent was explicitly asked to refactor two internal skills (hermes, hive-dispatch) — splitting them into a procedures-only `SKILL.md` and a companion reference file. The `skill-creator` skill was never loaded. As a result:

1. The agent placed `REFERENCE.md` in the **skill root** instead of the spec-required `references/` subdirectory
2. The agent added `SKILL.md`-style frontmatter to the reference file (invalid — `name` must match the parent directory name, and reference files aren't skills)
3. File references in `SKILL.md` used `./REFERENCE.md` instead of `references/REFERENCE.md`

All three issues are caught by the `skill-creator` guidance and the [AgentSkills spec](https://agentskills.io/specification) — but neither was consulted because the skill never triggered.

## Root cause

**Meta-skills have a triggering handicap.** The agent's attention is on the object-level task domain ("fix Hermes"), not the meta level ("I am currently authoring a skill file"). Meta-skill descriptions need unusually concrete trigger language to punch through.

Additionally, the agent arrived with a pre-formed plan ("split the file") rather than a discovery task. Skill-creator's value proposition leans toward discovery (understand → plan → init → edit → package), making it feel like overhead when the edit is already decided.

## Suggested fix

**1. Improve the description to cover edit/refactor explicitly:**

```
Create, update, or refactor AgentSkills. Use when: creating a new skill from scratch, editing or improving an existing SKILL.md, restructuring a skill's layout (splitting content, adding reference files, reorganizing scripts/assets), or validating a skill against the AgentSkills spec.
```

**2. Add an early trigger heuristic in the body:**

Add a note near the top of the SKILL.md body: "If you are about to write to any `SKILL.md` file or create/move files within a skill directory — you are doing skill authoring. Follow this guide."

**3. (Optional) Consider a filesystem-level trigger** if OpenClaw supports auto-loading a skill when certain file patterns are opened for editing.

## References

- [AgentSkills specification](https://agentskills.io/specification) — defines `references/` subdirectory layout, frontmatter constraints, naming rules
- Relevant spec excerpt: *"Skills should be structured for efficient use of context... Keep individual reference files focused. Agents load these on demand, so smaller files mean less use of context."*
title: skill-creator: description too narrow — fails to trigger on edit/refactor tasks

body:
## Problem
Memory index SQLite database (`~/.openclaw/memory/main.sqlite`) is created with `journal_mode=delete` (SQLite default). This causes frequent database corruption when the gateway receives SIGTERM during write operations (restart, update, config changes).

## Impact
- Users see "database disk image is malformed" errors
- Memory search stops working until manual `rm + openclaw memory index`
- Happens repeatedly since gateway restarts are common

## Root Cause
`journal_mode=delete` removes the journal file after each transaction. If the process is killed between deleting the journal and completing the write, the database corrupts.

## Suggested Fix
Set `PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL;` when creating or opening the memory database. WAL mode:
- Is crash-safe (survives SIGTERM/SIGKILL mid-write)
- Allows concurrent reads during writes
- Is the recommended mode for applications that restart frequently

```js
// After opening the database connection:
db.pragma('journal_mode = WAL');
```

## Workaround
```bash
sqlite3 ~/.openclaw/memory/main.sqlite "PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL;"
```
But this resets if the DB is recreated (e.g., after `openclaw memory index` on a corrupted DB).

## Environment
- OpenClaw 2026.3.2
- macOS arm64
- SQLite via better-sqlite3
title: Memory SQLite should use WAL journal mode by default

body:
## Problem

Feishu DM inbound can be processed successfully by the gateway/agent, but no reply is delivered to the Feishu client when the turn emits only `block` payloads and no `final` payload.

Symptoms observed in production:

- Inbound DM is logged and routed.
- Agent session contains assistant text.
- Feishu dispatch log shows `queuedFinal=false, replies=0`.
- Feishu client receives nothing.

## Root Cause

In `extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts`, `deliver()` returns early for `info.kind === "block"` unless `(streamingEnabled && useCard)`.

So for non-card block chunks (or block-only turns), text/media are dropped entirely if no final payload follows.

## Expected

If no final payload arrives, block payloads should still be delivered at idle as a fallback.

## Fix proposal

Buffer dropped block payloads (text/media) and flush on `onIdle` when no final payload was seen in the turn.

## PR

Opened PR with fix and test:

- https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/36032
title: Feishu: block-only turns can drop replies (dispatch ends with replies=0)

body:
## Problem

When the Matrix homeserver returns `M_LIMIT_EXCEEDED` with `retry_after_ms`, OpenClaw does not retry the send and the message is permanently lost.

## Reproduction

1. Self-hosted Synapse with default rate limiting
2. Agent processes a long-running request (10+ minutes due to API timeouts/retries)
3. Agent generates reply and attempts to send
4. Synapse returns `M_LIMIT_EXCEEDED` with `retry_after_ms: 1120` (1.1 seconds)
5. OpenClaw logs `[matrix] final reply failed: Error: M_LIMIT_EXCEEDED: Too Many Requests` and drops the message

## Observed logs

```
2026-03-05T08:56:31.667Z [diagnostic] lane wait exceeded: lane=session:agent:code:matrix:direct:@vitaliy:m.neomentor.tech waitedMs=743990 queueAhead=0
2026-03-05T11:56:36.733+03:00 MatrixHttpClient (REQ-2382) {
  errcode: 'M_LIMIT_EXCEEDED',
  error: 'Too Many Requests',
  retry_after_ms: 1120
}
2026-03-05T08:56:36.746Z [matrix] final reply failed: Error: M_LIMIT_EXCEEDED: Too Many Requests
```

## Expected behavior

When the homeserver returns `M_LIMIT_EXCEEDED` with `retry_after_ms`, OpenClaw should wait the specified time and retry (at least once). The `retry_after_ms` value is typically small (1-2 seconds), so this would not noticeably delay delivery.

## Environment

- OpenClaw: 2026.3.2
- Matrix plugin: @openclaw/matrix 2026.3.2
- Homeserver: Synapse (self-hosted)
- E2EE: enabled

## Related

- #29126 (general plugin delivery failures are silent/unrecoverable)
- The built-in Telegram channel likely handles rate limits internally; Matrix should follow the same pattern.
title: [Matrix] No retry on M_LIMIT_EXCEEDED — message lost permanently

body:
## Description

Health-monitor detects Discord channel connections as "stuck" and repeatedly restarts them, causing `Gateway is draining` errors for agents attempting to send messages during the restart window. This creates a cycle where connections are restarted every ~10 minutes.

## Environment

- **OpenClaw version**: 2026.3.2 (latest stable)
- **OS**: macOS (arm64) + Ubuntu (Hetzner VPS, 503GB RAM)
- **Setup**: Multi-agent gateway with 4 Discord bots (macOS) / 10 Discord bots (Ubuntu)
- **Channels**: Discord + Telegram per agent

## Symptoms

### 1. Health-monitor restart thrashing

Every ~10 minutes, health-monitor restarts Discord connections with `reason: stuck`:

```
[health-monitor] [discord:livius] health-monitor: restarting (reason: stuck)
[health-monitor] [discord:artemis] health-monitor: restarting (reason: stuck)
[health-monitor] [discord:onyx] health-monitor: restarting (reason: stuck)
```

Telegram connections also restart with `reason: stale-socket` at similar intervals.

**Scale (4-hour window):**

| Host | Health-monitor restarts | Slow listeners |
|------|------------------------|----------------|
| macOS (4 bots) | ~15 | ~4 |
| Ubuntu (10 bots) | **145** | **1,025** |

### 2. Root cause: DiscordMessageListener blocks event queue

A single `MESSAGE_CREATE` event blocks the listener for **minutes**:

```
[discord/monitor] Slow listener detected: DiscordMessageListener took 483517ms (483.5 seconds) for MESSAGE_CREATE
[discord/monitor] Slow listener detected: DiscordMessageListener took 48153ms (48.2 seconds) for MESSAGE_CREATE
```

This appears to happen when an agent processes an LLM inference synchronously within the Discord message listener, blocking the entire event queue. While the inference runs, health-monitor sees no activity and marks the connection as "stuck."

### 3. DiscordReactionListener slow warnings

Additionally, `DiscordReactionListener` and `DiscordReactionRemoveListener` regularly exceed 1-2.4 seconds:

```
[EventQueue] Slow listener detected: DiscordReactionListener took 2398ms for MESSAGE_REACTION_ADD
[EventQueue] Slow listener detected: DiscordReactionRemoveListener took 1580ms for MESSAGE_REACTION_REMOVE
```

On the Ubuntu host, 1,025 slow listener warnings in 4 hours (~4 per minute).

### 4. Agent impact: "Gateway is draining"

When health-monitor restarts a connection, any agent trying to use that Discord connection gets:

```
Gateway is draining, please try again later
```

This disrupts agent workflows mid-task.

## Expected behavior

1. LLM inference within Discord message handling should not block the event queue (async processing)
2. Health-monitor should distinguish between "connection dead" and "connection busy processing a long-running request"
3. The grace window added in v2026.3.2 (#32367) helps but doesn't fully solve the issue when inference takes 48-483 seconds

## Possible solutions

1. **Process LLM inference asynchronously** - decouple the Discord event listener from the LLM response pipeline so the event queue stays responsive
2. **Extend health-monitor awareness** - if a session is actively processing (LLM inference in progress), don't mark the connection as stuck
3. **Configurable stuck timeout** - allow operators to set a longer threshold for multi-agent setups where long inferences are expected

## Additional context

- The v2026.3.2 changelog mentions: "prevent health-monitor restart thrash for channels that just (re)started by adding a per-channel startup-connect grace window" (#32367) - this helps post-restart but doesn't prevent the initial "stuck" detection during long inferences
- Both hosts run the same version (2026.3.2) with identical behavior
- 56 zombie processes (defunct) accumulate on the Ubuntu host from repeated restarts (oldest from Feb 27)
- Potentially related: the zombie process accumulation suggests the restart flow doesn't fully clean up child processes
title: Health-monitor falsely detects Discord connections as 'stuck' during LLM inference, causing restart thrash and 'Gateway is draining' errors

body:
## Summary
When using gemini-3.1-pro-preview via OpenRouter in OpenClaw, the model exhibited two critical issues:
1. Instruction misinterpretation: Failed to understand delegation context
2. Repetitive output loop: Generated thousands of repeated sentences

## Environment
- Model: openrouter/google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview
- Temperature: 0.3
- Platform: OpenClaw
- Date: March 5, 2026

## Issue 1: Instruction Misinterpretation

When a user forwards an email to the AI assistant, the assistant should PROCESS the email (forward, archive, take action) - NOT send a summary back to the user.

Gemini 3.1 Pro sent summaries back for emails the user had just forwarded. The model failed to distinguish between informing vs delegating.

## Issue 2: Repetitive Output Loop

The model generated massively repetitive output - same sentence repeated hundreds of times (50k+ chars). No self-correction.

## Mitigation
- Reduced temperature 0.3 to 0.1
- Switched to Claude Opus for complex tasks

## Note
This is likely an upstream Google Gemini issue, reporting here for OpenClaw user visibility.
title: [Model Behavior] Gemini 3.1 Pro: Instruction misinterpretation + repetitive output loops

body:
## Description

Intermittent `TypeError: fetch failed` when downloading photos from Telegram media groups (multiple photos sent at once). Single photo messages work fine.

## Environment
- **OpenClaw**: 2026.3.1 (stable)
- **Node.js**: 25.6.1 (arm64, macOS 26.3)
- **OS**: macOS Sequoia 26.3 (Apple Silicon)

## Error
```
[telegram] media group: skipping photo that failed to fetch: MediaFetchError: Failed to fetch media from https://api.telegram.org/file/bot<token>/photos/file_XXXX.jpg: TypeError: fetch failed
```

## Frequency
- 5 failures across 8 days (Feb 26 – Mar 4)
- Only happens with media groups (multiple photos), never single photo messages
- 1 out of N photos in a group fails, rest download fine

## Root cause suspicion
Node.js 25 undici HTTP client + IPv6 resolution. When multiple concurrent fetches hit `api.telegram.org`, one occasionally fails due to IPv6/IPv4 race condition.

## Workarounds applied (partial fix)
1. `/etc/hosts`: `149.154.166.110 api.telegram.org` (force IPv4)
2. `NODE_OPTIONS=--dns-result-order=ipv4first` in LaunchAgent plist
3. Patched all `ssrf-*.js` files: added `family: 4` to dispatcher options
4. These reduced frequency but didn't eliminate it completely

## Related
- #24854 (IPv6 media download bug)

## Suggestion
Consider adding a retry mechanism for media group photo downloads — if one fetch fails, retry once before skipping. This would eliminate the user-visible error for intermittent network issues.
title: Telegram media group fetch intermittently fails on Node.js 25 (undici IPv6)

body:
## Summary
`/status` can show an incorrect context window when multiple providers expose the same model id with different limits.

## Repro
1. Configure two providers with the same model id (for example `claude-sonnet`) but different context windows.
2. Run a session on each provider.
3. Check `/status` and compare displayed context windows.

## Expected
When provider is known, `/status` should use that provider's context window.

## Actual
A shared bare-id cache can collapse to the smallest discovered window across providers.

## PR
Proposed fix: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/36002
title: Status summary should resolve context windows by provider-qualified model key

body:
### Bug type

Regression (worked before, now fails)

### Summary

## Description

When chatting via iMessage, assistant replies are now appearing in BOTH:
1. The original iMessage conversation
2. The Control UI / WebChat interface

This is a regression. Previously, these were independent - Control UI was just a viewer, replies only went to the source channel.

## Expected Behavior

- When user messages via iMessage, replies should ONLY go back to iMessage
- Control UI should only display the conversation, not receive duplicate replies

## Environment

- OpenClaw version: 2026.3.2
- macOS: 26.3
- Channels: iMessage enabled, Control UI (WebChat)

## Configuration

```json
{
  "channels": {
    "imessage": {
      "enabled": true
    }
  },

body:
### Summary

Exec approval prompts in Slack currently render as plain text requiring users to type `/approve <id> allow-once|allow-always|deny`. This is high-friction — especially on mobile, where copy-pasting a UUID into a slash command is painful.

### Problem to solve

When an elevated exec request needs approval, the gateway sends a plain text message:

```
🔒 Exec approval required
ID: ca6c42da-c9c3-...
Command: ls /home/user/...
CWD: /home/user/.openclaw/workspace
Expires in: 120s
Reply with: /approve <id> allow-once|allow-always|deny
```

Users must manually type the `/approve` command with the correct ID and decision.

### Proposed solution

Render approval prompts using [Slack Block Kit](https://api.slack.com/block-kit) with interactive buttons:

```
🔒 Exec approval required

Command: `ls /home/user/...`
CWD: /home/user/.openclaw/workspace
Agent: main | Security: full | Expires in: 120s

[✅ Allow Once]  [✅ Allow Always]  [❌ Deny]
```
Each button resolves the approval via exec.approval.resolve internally — no slash command needed.

Slack Block Kit actions block with three button elements
action_id encodes the approval ID + decision
Requires handling Slack's block_actions interaction payload via the existing socket mode connection
Should fall back to the current plain text format for non-Slack channels or if the Slack app lacks interactions scope
The buildRequestMessage() function in the gateway approval logic would need a channel-aware render path

### Alternatives considered

- Slack slash command (current): Works, but high friction. Requires typing /approve <uuid> <decision> — error-prone, slow on mobile, and the approval ID isn't user-friendly.

- Slack reactions (emoji-based): Lower friction than slash commands, but limited to a single action per emoji, ambiguous semantics (what does 👍 mean — allow-once or allow-always?), and not all Slack clients surface reactions prominently.

- External approval dashboard (web UI): The Control UI and macOS app already handle this, but many users (especially self-hosted Linux deployments) interact primarily through chat channels and shouldn't need a separate app open to approve commands.

### Impact

- Affected: All self-hosted operators using Slack as their primary approval channel
- Severity: Blocks workflow — approval timeouts cause exec requests to be denied, halting agent work
- Frequency: Daily (19 approval timeouts in 48 hours on a single-user deployment)
- Consequence: Agent stalls for 120s per unanswered request, then fails. On mobile, typing `/approve <uuid> allow-once` is slow enough that timeouts are common even when the user IS available.

### Evidence/examples

- 19 exec approval timeouts observed in 48 hours on a real self-hosted deployment (single operator, Slack socket mode). All timed out at 120s because the text-based /approve workflow is too slow to catch.
- Feishu channel already has interactive card parsing (#34571), establishing precedent for channel-specific rich message formats in the codebase.
- Slack's Workflow Builder uses interactive buttons for its own approval steps — this is the established platform UX pattern users expect.

### Additional information

- Affected code: buildRequestMessage() and buildResolvedMessage() in gateway exec approval routing, plus the Slack channel plugin for handling block_actions payloads.

- Backward compatible: channels without Block Kit support (Telegram, Discord, IRC) continue receiving plain text — no breaking change.

- Scope can be kept small: only the Slack channel plugin needs the interactive path; all other channels stay as-is.
title: [Feature]: (Slack) Interactive Block Kit buttons for exec approval prompts

### Bug type

### Summary

**Description**
When using a third-party Anthropic API relay (proxy) that returns responses with extended thinking enabled, OpenClaw fails to parse the response and shows:

LLM error api_error: 上游返回了 19 个事件但未产生有效输出，请重试
Simple queries (without tools/system prompts) work fine, but any complex query that triggers tool use or requires extended reasoning fails consistently.

OS: Ubuntu 22.04.5 LTS (x86_64)

json
{
  "models": {
    "mode": "merge",
    "providers": {
      "nexus": {
        "baseUrl": "https://example-relay.cc/api",
        "apiKey": "sk-xxx",
        "api": "anthropic-messages",
        "models": [
          {
            "id": "claude-opus-4-6",
            "name": "Claude Opus 4.6",
            "contextWindow": 180000,
            "maxTokens": 8192
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}

### Steps to reproduce

Configure OpenClaw with a third-party Anthropic API relay that has extended thinking enabled by default
The relay returns "model": "claude-opus-4-6-thinking" in responses (appends -thinking suffix)
Send a simple message like "今天几号？" → ✅ Works fine
Send a complex message that triggers tool use, e.g. "今天科技圈最爆炸的是什么？" → ❌ Fails

### Expected behavior

OpenClaw should either:

Correctly parse SSE event streams that contain thinking content blocks alongside 
text
 content blocks
Or gracefully handle the case where the relay returns thinking events, extracting the actual text output

### Actual behavior

OpenClaw receives the streamed response (e.g., 17-19 SSE events), but fails to extract any valid text output from them. The error message indicates events were received but no usable content was found:

embedded run agent end: runId=xxx isError=true error=LLM error api_error: 上游返回了 17 个事件但未产生有效输出，请重试

body:
# Incident Record: Intermittent `Invalid diff: now finding less tool calls!` (Max/Jason)

- Date: 2026-03-05 (Asia/Taipei)
- Reporter: Marcus
- Impact: Intermittent task failures in Max/Jason paths; noisy/error outputs despite successful runs before/after.

## Symptom
- Error string:
  - `Invalid diff: now finding less tool calls!`
- Seen mainly in:
  - Max and Jason execution paths
  - Cron and DM-related execution contexts

## Confirmed Evidence
- Max cron job (new job without model override):
  - Job: `707ec10b-c6bc-4f8c-9bbb-46d6df8dd037`
  - 17:00 run failed with:
    - status: `error`
    - summary/error: `Invalid diff: now finding less tool calls!`
    - sessionKey: `agent:max:cron:707ec10b-c6bc-4f8c-9bbb-46d6df8dd037:run:9a50c04c-af50-436d-b6f0-1e4f057ba625`
  - Neighbor runs (16:30 / 17:30 / 18:00 / 18:30): `ok`
- System log correlation:
  - Repeated Discord channel health monitor restarts for `discord:max` (reason `stuck`)
  - Repeated slow listener signals (`DiscordMessageListener` large durations)

## Important Context
- Old cron jobs using `payload.model=toolbox/qwen3-coder` were migrated and disabled.
- New replacement jobs were created without explicit `payload.model` override.
- Error still appears intermittently after migration.

## Hypothesis
Likely runtime/tool-call diff instability under specific conditions (local model + tool-calling path + event/listener pressure), not merely user cron misconfiguration.

## Mitigations Applied
1. Migrated cron jobs away from hardcoded invalid model override.
2. Restarted gateway and executed smoke runs (successful immediately after restart).
3. Replaced noisy Max calendar job with shorter prompt + fresh session key:
   - New job: `e0eb72c1-735d-4ff8-9426-37eddacd9d65`
   - Old job disabled.
4. Added monitor job for alert only on consecutive failures:
   - `fcf20a5d-f1fd-4b90-8b69-287b3d02828d`

## Repro Clues for Maintainers
- Intermittent nature: fails one run, succeeds surrounding runs.
- Seen on Max/Jason paths more than Ruby/David.
- Co-occurs with Discord `stuck` restarts and listener slowdown.

## Suggested Next Diagnostics (upstream)
- Add richer tracing around tool-call diff application when model output changes tool-call count between partial/final chunks.
- Correlate per-run model response chunks with event-loop lag and listener durations.
- Validate handling for local model provider outputs that may emit unstable/incomplete tool-call structures.
title: Intermittent 'Invalid diff: now finding less tool calls!' on Max/Jason paths (after cron model override migration)
